meta.title: GraphQL
meta.description: Review checklist for GraphQL schema, resolvers, and API design

items.text: Do type and field names follow GraphQL naming conventions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: GraphQL conventions: PascalCase for types (User, OrderConnection), camelCase for fields (firstName, createdAt), SCREAMING_SNAKE for enums (ORDER_STATUS).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent naming makes the schema predictable and easy to use. Clients shouldn't have to guess whether it's userId or user_id.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are types PascalCase? Fields camelCase? Enum values SCREAMING_SNAKE? Are abbreviations avoided (use 'identifier' not 'id' for clarity)?
baseContent.exampleComment: The field `user_name` should be `userName` per GraphQL convention (camelCase for fields).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow GraphQL conventions: PascalCase types, camelCase fields, SCREAMING_SNAKE enums.

items.text: Is nullability intentional for every field (! used appropriately)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: In GraphQL, fields are nullable by default. Non-null (!) should be used when a field is guaranteed to have a value. Getting this wrong breaks clients.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Making a field non-null then returning null crashes the entire response (null propagates up). Making everything nullable forces clients to handle nulls everywhere.
baseContent.howToVerify: For each field: is the data always available? Can it fail independently? Is it worth propagating a null error to the parent?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `user.email` field is non-null (!) but email can be null for social-login users. Remove the ! or ensure it's always populated.

codeExamples.label: Intentional nullability

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to nullable. Only use ! when you're sure the field can never be null, including error cases.

items.text: Are mutations using Input types (not inline arguments)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Mutations with multiple arguments should use a dedicated Input type instead of many individual arguments.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Input types are easier to evolve (add optional fields), document, and reuse. They also make client code cleaner.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does a mutation have more than 2-3 arguments? Should they be grouped into an input type?
baseContent.exampleComment: This mutation has 8 inline arguments. Create a `CreateOrderInput` type to group them.

codeExamples.label: Input type

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Input types for mutations with more than 2-3 parameters. They're easier to evolve.

items.text: Do types and fields have descriptions for documentation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: GraphQL schemas are self-documenting through introspection. Descriptions (doc strings) appear in tools like GraphiQL and Apollo Studio.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without descriptions, developers using the API must guess what fields mean, leading to misuse and support burden.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do new types and non-obvious fields have description strings? Are enum values documented?
baseContent.exampleComment: Add descriptions to the `OrderStatus` enum values so API consumers know the difference between PROCESSING and PENDING.

codeExamples.label: Documented schema
codeExamples.code:
"""A customer order with line items and shipping details."""
type Order {
  """Unique identifier for the order."""
  id: ID!
  """Current status of the order in the fulfillment pipeline."""
  status: OrderStatus!
}

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Document your schema. Descriptions appear in introspection and are your API's documentation.

items.text: Are enums used instead of String for fixed sets of values?

baseContent.whatItMeans: GraphQL enums provide type safety and documentation for fields with a known set of values. Strings allow any value.
baseContent.whyItMatters: String-typed status fields accept invalid values like typos. Enums catch these at the schema level and document valid options.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are there String fields that only accept specific values? Should they be enums?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `status` field is a String but only accepts 'ACTIVE', 'INACTIVE', 'SUSPENDED'. Use an enum type.

codeExamples.label: Enum type

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use enums for finite value sets. They provide validation and documentation for free.

items.text: Is pagination using the Relay Connection pattern (edges/nodes/pageInfo)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The Relay Connection specification provides a standard way to paginate with cursors, supporting forward/backward navigation and total counts.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Custom pagination patterns vary between teams and make client libraries harder to use. The Connection pattern is widely supported.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is list pagination using Connection types with edges, nodes, pageInfo? Or a custom approach? Does the team use Relay?
baseContent.exampleComment: This list field returns `[User!]!` with no pagination. Use a Connection type for lists that could be large.

codeExamples.label: Connection pattern

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the Connection pattern for paginated lists. It's the industry standard.

items.text: Are N+1 queries prevented with DataLoader or batching?

baseContent.whatItMeans: GraphQL's nested resolution naturally causes N+1 queries: fetching a list of orders, then resolving the user for each order individually.
baseContent.whyItMatters: N+1 queries are the #1 GraphQL performance problem. 50 orders loading 50 users means 51 queries instead of 2.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are related entities loaded individually in resolvers? Is DataLoader used for batching? Are SQL JOINs used where appropriate?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `Order.user` resolver makes a separate DB query for each order. Use DataLoader to batch these into a single `WHERE id IN (...)` query.

codeExamples.label: DataLoader

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use DataLoader for every resolver that fetches related entities. It's the standard solution for N+1 in GraphQL.

items.text: Are resolver errors handled consistently (not leaking internal details)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Resolver errors should use GraphQL error extensions, not expose stack traces or database error messages to clients.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Leaking internal error details helps attackers. Inconsistent error formats make client error handling fragile.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do resolvers catch errors and return structured GraphQL errors? Are database errors and stack traces filtered out?
baseContent.exampleComment: This resolver throws the raw Prisma error message to the client, revealing database schema details. Wrap it in a user-friendly error.

codeExamples.label: Error handling

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never expose internal errors to clients. Use error codes and user-friendly messages.

items.text: Is authorization checked at the resolver level?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Authorization must be checked in resolvers, not just at the query/mutation entry point. Nested resolvers can expose unauthorized data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A user querying their own order can traverse to another user's data through relationships if nested resolvers don't check permissions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does each resolver verify the user has access to the requested data? Are there nested fields that could expose other users' data?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `Order.user` resolver returns any user without checking if the requester should see that user's details. Add authorization.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Check authorization in every resolver that returns sensitive data. Don't rely on parent resolvers alone.

items.text: Is the resolver context used correctly (not storing mutable state)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The GraphQL context should contain per-request data (user, DataLoaders) but should not be mutated during resolution.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mutating context during resolution can cause race conditions in async resolvers and make behavior order-dependent.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is the context object modified during resolution? Are DataLoaders created per-request (not shared across requests)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This resolver adds data to `context.cache` during resolution. Use DataLoader instead — it handles batching and caching correctly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Context is for reading per-request data. Use DataLoader for caching and batching within a request.

items.text: Are field resolvers efficient (not over-fetching from the database)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Field resolvers should only fetch data when the field is actually requested. Eagerly loading all relationships wastes resources.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If every User resolver pre-loads orders, friends, and settings, queries that only need the user's name waste significant resources.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do resolvers eagerly load related data? Could expensive fields use lazy resolution or check if they're in the selection set?
baseContent.exampleComment: The User resolver always JOINs orders and settings even when only `user.name` is queried. Use separate field resolvers for nested data.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Resolve fields lazily. Only fetch what's requested in the query.

items.text: Is query depth limited to prevent deeply nested attacks?

baseContent.whatItMeans: GraphQL allows infinitely nested queries (user → friends → friends → friends...). Without depth limiting, this enables DoS attacks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A malicious query with 100 levels of nesting can exponentially increase database queries and crash the server.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is there a depth limit configured? What's the maximum allowed depth? Does it account for legitimate deep queries?
baseContent.exampleComment: There's no query depth limit. An attacker could send `{ user { friends { friends { friends { ... } } } } }` to crash the server. Add a depth limit of 10.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set a query depth limit (typically 7-15). Use libraries like graphql-depth-limit.

items.text: Is query complexity/cost analysis configured?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Query complexity analysis assigns costs to fields and rejects queries exceeding a threshold. This prevents expensive queries.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without cost analysis, a single query can request all fields on all types with maximum pagination, overwhelming the server.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is there a complexity limit? Are expensive fields (lists, computed fields) assigned higher costs?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `allOrders` field with no complexity cost can return 10000 orders with all nested relations. Assign a cost multiplier based on the limit argument.

codeExamples.label: Complexity config

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Assign costs to fields, especially lists. Reject queries that exceed the budget.

items.text: Is introspection disabled in production?

baseContent.whatItMeans: GraphQL introspection reveals your entire schema — every type, field, and relationship. This is valuable for attackers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Introspection in production lets attackers map your entire API surface, find hidden fields, and craft targeted attacks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is introspection explicitly disabled in the production configuration? Is it only enabled in development?
baseContent.exampleComment: Introspection is enabled in production. Disable it with `introspection: false` in the Apollo Server config.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Disable introspection in production. Use schema registries for tooling instead.

items.text: Is rate limiting applied per operation or per complexity?

baseContent.whatItMeans: GraphQL's single endpoint makes traditional rate limiting insufficient. Rate limiting should consider query complexity, not just request count.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A single complex GraphQL query can do the work of 100 REST requests. Rate limiting by request count doesn't prevent abuse.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is rate limiting configured? Does it account for query complexity or just request count? Are mutations rate-limited differently?
baseContent.exampleComment: Rate limiting allows 100 requests/minute, but each request can be an expensive query. Consider complexity-based rate limiting.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Rate limit by query complexity, not just request count. Mutations may need stricter limits.

items.text: Are persisted queries used to prevent arbitrary query execution?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Persisted queries allow only pre-registered queries to execute, preventing attackers from crafting malicious ad-hoc queries.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without persisted queries, any client can send any query, including expensive or exploratory ones that probe your schema.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the production server accept arbitrary queries or only pre-registered ones? Is automatic persisted queries (APQ) configured?
baseContent.exampleComment: Consider using persisted queries in production to prevent arbitrary query execution. Apollo supports Automatic Persisted Queries.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Persisted queries lock down what can be executed. Use them in production for defense in depth.

items.text: Are list fields paginated (not returning unbounded arrays)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: List fields without pagination can return thousands of items, overwhelming the client and server.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An unbounded `users` query returning 1M records crashes the server, exhausts memory, and times out the client.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do list fields have `first`/`after` or `limit`/`offset` arguments? Is there a maximum page size enforced?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `orders` field returns all orders with no pagination. Add `first` and `after` arguments with a max of 100.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every list field needs pagination. Enforce a maximum page size server-side.

items.text: Is there a caching strategy for frequently-accessed data?

baseContent.whatItMeans: GraphQL responses benefit from caching at multiple levels: CDN (with cache control), DataLoader (per-request), and application cache (Redis).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without caching, every query hits the database. For public data like product catalogs, this is unnecessary and slow.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is cache-control configured for public queries? Are DataLoaders caching within requests? Is Redis used for cross-request caching?
baseContent.exampleComment: This product catalog query hits the database every time. Add @cacheControl(maxAge: 300) for 5-minute CDN caching.

codeExamples.label: Cache control

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Cache public data aggressively. Use DataLoader for per-request deduplication.

items.text: Are related mutations batched instead of requiring multiple round trips?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Instead of requiring clients to call 10 individual mutations, provide batch mutations that handle multiple items in one request.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Multiple round trips add latency and increase the chance of partial failures. Batch operations are more efficient and atomic.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the client need to make multiple mutation calls that could be a single batch? Is there a `createMany` or `bulkUpdate` mutation?
baseContent.exampleComment: The client calls `addItemToCart` 5 times in a loop. Provide an `addItemsToCart(items: [CartItemInput!]!)` mutation for efficiency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Provide batch mutations for operations that clients commonly perform in loops.

items.text: Are resolvers using query analysis to optimize data fetching?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Resolvers can inspect the GraphQL selection set to determine which fields are requested and optimize database queries accordingly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without query analysis, resolvers may eagerly load data that the client didn't request, wasting database and network resources.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do resolvers check which fields are selected? Could the database query be optimized based on the requested fields?
baseContent.exampleComment: This resolver always JOINs 5 tables regardless of which fields are queried. Use the info argument to build targeted SQL.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the selection set to build optimized database queries. Only fetch what's requested.

items.text: Are error codes standardized and documented?

baseContent.whatItMeans: GraphQL errors should use standardized extension codes that clients can programmatically handle.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without error codes, clients can only parse error messages (which change). Codes provide stable, machine-readable error categories.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do errors include extension codes? Are codes documented? Do clients use codes (not messages) for error handling?
baseContent.exampleComment: This error only has a message. Add an extensions.code so clients can handle it programmatically: `{ code: 'NOT_FOUND' }`.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use error codes for machine handling, messages for humans. Document both.

items.text: Are partial responses (data + errors) handled correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: GraphQL can return both data and errors in the same response. Nullable fields that error return null alongside the error.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Clients that check only for errors and ignore data (or vice versa) miss valid partial responses.
baseContent.howToVerify: If a nested resolver fails, does the response correctly null the field and include the error? Do clients handle partial data?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `User.recentOrders` resolver can fail independently. Make sure the schema allows it to be nullable so partial responses work.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design for partial responses. Nullable fields can fail independently without breaking the entire query.

items.text: Are input validation errors clear and actionable?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Validation errors should tell the user which field failed, why, and what values are acceptable.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Generic 'Invalid input' errors force users to guess what's wrong. Field-specific errors enable better client-side UX.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do validation errors specify which field is invalid? Do they explain the constraint? Can the client map errors to form fields?
baseContent.exampleComment: This validation error says 'Invalid input'. Specify the field: `{ field: 'email', message: 'Must be a valid email address' }`.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validation errors should name the field, explain the rule, and be machine-parseable.

items.text: Are removed fields deprecated first with a reason?

baseContent.whatItMeans: GraphQL supports @deprecated directive for fields that will be removed. Deprecated fields should remain functional during a migration period.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Removing a field without deprecation breaks all clients using it. Deprecation gives clients time to migrate.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is a field being removed? Is it deprecated first with a reason? Is the replacement documented?
baseContent.exampleComment: Don't remove the `fullName` field yet. Deprecate it first: `fullName: String @deprecated(reason: "Use firstName and lastName")` and remove it next quarter.

codeExamples.label: Deprecation

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Deprecate before removing. Give clients a migration window with clear alternatives.

items.text: Are type changes backwards-compatible (nullable to non-null is breaking)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Certain schema changes are breaking: making nullable fields non-null, changing return types, removing enum values, changing argument types.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A breaking schema change crashes all clients that depend on the old behavior. In GraphQL, the schema IS the contract.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is a field's type changing? Is nullability changing? Is an enum value being removed? These are all breaking changes.
baseContent.exampleComment: Changing `email: String` to `email: String!` is a breaking change — clients handling null emails will break. Add a new non-null field instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Additive changes are safe. Removals and type changes are breaking. Use schema change detection tools.

items.text: Is there automated schema change detection in CI?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tools like graphql-inspector or Apollo Studio detect breaking changes by comparing the new schema against the published one.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Manual review of schema changes misses subtle breaking changes. Automated detection catches them before they reach production.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is there a CI check that compares schema changes? Does it flag breaking changes? Is the schema registered with a schema registry?
baseContent.exampleComment: Add graphql-inspector to CI to automatically detect breaking schema changes: `npx graphql-inspector diff old.graphql new.graphql`.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Automate breaking change detection. Don't rely on manual review for schema compatibility.

items.text: Do resolvers have unit tests?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Resolver logic should be unit tested with mocked data sources to verify business logic, error handling, and authorization.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untested resolvers silently break during refactoring. Unit tests catch logic errors before they reach integration tests.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are there unit tests for the new/modified resolvers? Do they test happy path, error cases, and authorization?
baseContent.exampleComment: This resolver has complex authorization logic but no unit tests. Add tests for: authorized access, unauthorized access, and edge cases.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test resolvers like any other function. Mock the data layer, verify the business logic.

items.text: Are integration tests using actual GraphQL queries?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Integration tests should send real GraphQL queries to verify the full stack: parsing, validation, resolution, and serialization.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unit tests miss integration issues: schema mismatches, resolver wiring, middleware ordering, and serialization bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are there integration tests that send GraphQL queries and verify responses? Do they cover the main query patterns?
baseContent.exampleComment: Add an integration test that sends the `createOrder` mutation and verifies the response matches the expected shape.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Integration tests should use actual GraphQL queries, not just test resolvers in isolation.

items.text: Is there a schema snapshot test to detect unintended changes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Schema snapshot tests compare the current schema against a saved snapshot, catching unintended changes during development.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without snapshots, accidental schema changes (like removing a field during refactoring) go unnoticed until production.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is there a snapshot test for the schema? Is it updated intentionally when the schema changes?
baseContent.exampleComment: Add a schema snapshot test so accidental schema changes are caught in CI.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Snapshot your schema. Intentional changes update the snapshot; accidental changes fail the test.
